content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
August25, 1997
James Sutton
Nielsen, Merksamer, Parrinello, Mueller & Naylor, LLP
591 Redwood Highway, #4000
Mill Valley, California 94941
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-97-376
Dear Mr. Sutton:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf ofthe California Republican
Party regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Because your request is
general in nature, we are treating it as one for informal assistance pursuant to regulation
18329(c).
2
Specifically, you want to know whether Proposition 208 changed the Commission’s
advice in the Fogarty Advice Letter, No. A-96-028. In that letter, the Commission advised the
AFL-CIO that the purchase of advertisement space for fair market value in a booklet distributed
by the Orange County Democratic Party at its yearly convention was not a contribution to the
Democratic Party.
The Commission’s conclusion was based on the term “contribution” as defined in the
Act. Section 82015 provides that a payment made in exchange for full and adequate
consideration is not a contribution. Because the union received full and adequate consideration
in the form of advertisement space, the payment to the Democratic Party did not constitute a
contribution. (See also Robeck Advice Letter, No. A-89-489.)
Proposition 208 amended various provisions of the Act. However, the initiative did not
modif’ the definition of contribution. Moreover, section 85202 provides that the definitions
enumerated in the Act will govern the interpretation of the provisions of Proposition 208. Thus,
Proposition 208 did not alter the Commission’s conclusion in the Fogarty advice letter.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2
.
.
.
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immumty provided by an opmion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-376
Page 2
Please note, however, the Commission’s conclusion in the Fogarty advice letter is limited
to its facts. The facts in that letter involved the long tradition of selling advertisement space in a
party convention booklet and the Commission advised on the issue of disclosure only. At the
time the letter was issued, there were no contribution limits in effect. Because the Commission
cannot condone the circumvention of contribution limits to political party committees, please be
aware that determinations on each specific factual situation will be made on a case-by-case basis.
Any claims that a committee is giving “fair market value” in return for large sums ofmoney will
be closely scrutinized by the Commission.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Graduate Legal Assistant, Legal Division
SGC:JB:tls

sections.question: You want to know whether Proposition 208 changed the Commission's advice in the Fogarty Advice Letter, No. A-96-028. In that letter, the Commission advised the AFL-CIO that the purchase of advertisement space for fair market value in a booklet distributed by the Orange County Democratic Party at its yearly convention was not a contribution to the Democratic Party.
sections.conclusion: Proposition 208 amended various provisions of the Act. However, the initiative did not modify the definition of contribution. Moreover, section 85202 provides that the definitions enumerated in the Act will govern the interpretation of the provisions of Proposition 208. Thus, Proposition 208 did not alter the Commission's conclusion in the Fogarty advice letter. However, the Commission's conclusion in the Fogarty advice letter is limited to its facts and determinations on each specific factual situation will be made on a case-by-case basis.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor asks whether Proposition 208 modified the FPPC's prior advice in the Fogarty letter regarding whether the purchase of advertisement space at fair market value in a political party convention booklet constitutes a contribution under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Proposition 208 did not change the definition of contribution or alter the Fogarty letter's conclusion that fair market value payments for advertisement space are not contributions. The FPPC warns, however, that determinations are made case-by-case and will closely scrutinize claims of 'fair market value' to prevent circumvention of contribution limits.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Informal advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Letter treats the matter as informal assistance under regulation 18329(c), noting limited immunity. References prior Fogarty Advice Letter (A-96-028) and Robeck Advice Letter (A-89-489).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You want to know whether Proposition 208 changed the Commission's advice in the Fogarty Advice Letter, No. A-96-028. In that letter, the Commission advised the AFL-CIO that the purchase of advertisement space for fair market value in a booklet distributed by the Orange County Democratic Party at its yearly convention was not a contribution to the Democratic Party.

CONCLUSION: Proposition 208 amended various provisions of the Act. However, the initiative did not modify the definition of contribution. Moreover, section 85202 provides that the definitions enumerated in the Act will govern the interpretation of the provisions of Proposition 208. Thus, Proposition 208 did not alter the Commission's conclusion in the Fogarty advice letter. However, the Commission's conclusion in the Fogarty advice letter is limited to its facts and determinations on each specific factual situation will be made on a case-by-case basis.

In that letter, the Commission advised the AFL-CIO that the purchase of advertisement space for fair market value in a booklet distributed by the Orange County Democratic Party at its yearly convention was not a contribution to the Democratic Party. Churchwell General Counsel By: Julia Butcher Graduate Legal Assistant, Legal Division SGC:JB:tls
embedding.summary: FPPC advises that Proposition 208 did not modify the definition of contribution in the Political Reform Act and therefore did not change the Commission's prior Fogarty letter advice that fair market value advertisement space purchases in party convention booklets are not contributions. The Commission cautions that each factual situation will be evaluated individually and claims of fair market value will be closely scrutinized to prevent contribution limit circumvention.
